questions.question:
A survey was conducted by a group of students as part of their environmental awareness project, in which they collected data regarding the number of plants in $20$ houses in a locality. The data is summarized in the following frequency distribution table:

| Number of plants | $0-2$ | $2-4$ | $4-6$ | $6-8$ | $8-10$ | $10-12$ | $12-14$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of houses ($f_i$) | $1$ | $2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $k$ | $2$ | $3$ |

The total number of houses is $20$.
What is the value of the missing frequency $k$?

steps: The sum of all frequencies is given as $20$.

questions.question: Find the class mark ($x_i$) for the class interval $8-10$.

steps: The class mark is the average of the upper and lower limits.

questions.question: Which method is most suitable to calculate the mean of this grouped data?

steps: Since the class intervals are uniform ($h=2$) and the frequencies/class marks are small, the Direct Method or Assumed Mean Method are both efficient.
Given the small numerical values, the Direct Method is straightforward and sufficient.
solution.derivation: \text{The values are small, hence the Direct Method } \bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} \text{ is efficient.}

questions.question: Calculate the mean number of plants per house.
